name: A Beautiful Company
comments:
-	Regional offices – I’m looking for potential off market / stressed situations to buy top 10 office buildings in the top 10 regions for c.10%. AG would be the capital behind this so we’re well funded.
-	Operational real estate – health care, NHS angle, government income, or anything else alternative with a PE angle. Looking to invest £100-200m+ so the bigger the better
o	Frogmore you said has some care homes?
-	Mayfair offices – let me know bid dates / progress on the 3 you sent me, or if I should reach out directly to the agent.
-	MIPIM – let me know when you’re having drinks and I’ll come join you!

name: AIG
comments:
Working slowly across the range at present, very BTR focused.

*Core-Plus
•	Cautiously working up core-plus capital but they are not quite there yet.
•	Likely to be later in the year if / when this is materialised.

**Co-Living
•	Lewis is focussed on UK and Ireland, but generally speaking they will look at co-living.
•	Prefer smaller lot sizes.

name: AXA
comments:
06.06.2022:

Meeting with John O’Driscoll who runs UK & Europe. Several pockets ranging from core, core plus to value add. Not shy on development as we know. Are hiring someone to help Stuart Carr Jones so they have more UK bandwith.

BTR not found a platform yet that works from a fees and pricing perspective. SF can’t do given reputational risk. Own Retirement Villages which are largely southern based and largely BTS so one of the seniors operators in the North or more advanced in BTR would be interesting.

Have a €4bn open ended fund for logistics that issues unsecured debt so very competitive. Own Data4, pan European DC developer and operator, looking for more UK sites. Like core offices, no interest in secondary.

name: Abdn
comments:
13/11/2025
Distribution and Logistics £15m to £100m
. Well located, flexible Logistics Units and Parcel Hubs with  strong market demand / supply dynamics.
. Urban Distribution / Collection facilities and last mile delivery units in key commercial centres.
. Quality Multi-Let Estates and Trade Parks in London, the South East and strong regional locations.
. Accessible locations with strong labour pools and power provision are of particular interest.
. Speculative opportunities from £10m - £30m, in strong locations with flexible specifications.
Offices £20m to £200m
. High quality, efficient and future-proofed offices which provide flexibility and additional service provision.
. Preference for multi-let, Freehold West End of London offices.
. Well connected strong regional and South East locations. London offices in strong sub-markets, including potential value-add opportunities.
Social Infrastructure £5m to £50m
. Primary Healthcare and related infrastructure assets.
. Life Sciences including laboratories, innovation centres and high-tech offices.
. Dedicated Data Centres in close proximity to major city locations.
. Public and private Educational Facilities with or without accommodation.
. Self-Storage, Car Parks, and Roadside Operations.
. Specialised Real Estate such as energy generation, waste transfer, crematoria, sustainable woodland, solar farms, and forestry.
Living £15m to £80m
. Stand alone or mixed-use Build to Rent opportunities.
. Serviced Apartments in Greater London and dominant city centre locations.
. Leased and direct let Student Accommodation in strong University locations.
. Leased and affordable Care Homes and Senior Living in high quality schemes.
. Modern Hotels and Aparthotels with OMV, turnover or index linked leases in strong locations.
Secure Income £15m to £150m
. Investment grade covenants for an average lease length in excess of 15 years.
. Preference for inflation linked or fixed increase review structures.
. Commercial Ground Rents with inflation linked review structures.
Supermarkets and Retail Warehousing £5m to £75m
. Right sized Supermarkets which offer omnichannel solutions with strong trading performance, let off affordable rents.
. Retail warehousing let off re-based rents and with a discount, food, or convenience orientated line up.

name: Angelo Gordon
comments:
Overview

•	Angelo Gordon has been investing in global credit and real estate markets for over 30 years. 
•	It is a leading, privately-held alternative investment firm, with AUM of $52 billion.
•	It invests on behalf of pension funds, corporations, endowments, foundations, sovereign wealth funds and individuals.  
•	The real estate arm targets assets in the U.S, Europe, and Asia, where they can employ value-add skills to improve property performance. 
•	It has been investing in real estate in Europe since 2009 and has acquired over $5 billion of properties in the region.
•	In addition, it invests in commercial real estate debt, and in net lease real estate (focusing on the sale/leaseback of corporate HQs). 
•	Works in tandem with their network of approximately 50 local operating partners.
•	Recently sold to AIMCo and Ridgeback (July 2022) a £283m resi portfolio, which they backed Ridgeback to develop.
o	MD of Europe Real Estate quoted as saying ‘we believe the opportunity set in the residential sector in the UK remains highly compelling and expect the asset class will continue to be an area where we deploy substantial capital.’

name: Areli
comments: Were interested in the retail element of Edmonton, with their UHNW pocket.

name: Ares
comments:
Ares have two /three vehicles 
•	Flagship – opportunistic fund – IRR driven multiple test – 18%+, 1.6 EM levered true net return back to ares post fees /tax. Zero restrictions on leverage and (Come in at any stage of the development cycle). Normally low 20’s but given product could be closer to collar.
•	Value add/core +- 12-15% IRR , 1.5 EM, Levered net return discretionary to Europe – quarterly distribution of 5%.  Restricted up to 60% and can only step in after the development a forward fund wouldn’t work and a grey area for a forward purchase. Sector agnostic.
•	US Core capital – allocation for Europe – high single digit returns net returns back to US , harder as lots of FX and fees to get it back.  
•Apply equal weighting to IRR and EM. 

•	Ares have capital to invest – from stretch senior through to opportunistic.
•	Real estate core + / value add product – require low double digit IRRs on equity side.
•	Historical opportunistic private equity real estate fund – requires 15% IRR and ‘can do anything’.
•	£1bn equity in each pot, therefore £4bn equity to invest, and would use debt on this.
•	A lot of capital to deploy
•	Interested in:
o	Resi, logistics / industrial, light industrial, retirement living, PBSA
o	Spent a good amount of time looking at retirement living but never been able to piece together
•	Like the rental angle, but is unsubstantiated with the premium people might / might not pay
•	Cost of dev hard to stack up atm due to cost of dev finance
•	Last 3-4 months, seen a lot of less 'in vogue' sectors where people need to plug gap (i.e. funding gap)
o	We can provide capital to plug this

name: Augur Group
comments:
Augur Group have immediate equity to deploy on behalf of a range of mandates ranging from Core + to Value-Add and Development.

Lot size: £3-15m+
Target return: 10%+
Hold: 3-7yrs
Geography: UK wide
Sectors: All
Strategy: AM/Development

 Strong secondary hotels – Leased or Management Contract
 Prime high street retail – ‘Top 10’ loca􀆟ons
 Dominant retail warehouse
 Core + offices – ERV c. £30+ (c. 25,0000 – 50,000 sq 􀅌)
 Leisure
 Secondary industrial
 Open Storage

name: Aviva Investors
comments:
Like single family, like platforms. Invested in UK affordable.
14/03/2023:
- WIP with Charlie Littlemore
- Works on long income transactions and Assistant Fund Manager on Aviva Life and Pens Fund 
- Aviva Life and Pens ultimate aim is to outpace MSCI by 50bps
- Essex County Council and UKGI - £1bn each
- Heritage Friends Life - managed by Ed Fuller - looking to track MSCI
- New fund (TBD name) - absolute return fund (6% net of fees)
- Not acquisitive til H2 this year
- Internal capital funds are acquisitive at the moment
- £400m capital to deploy this year - sat on £830m cash atm
- £35-£80/90m lot size on single assets, can go higher for portfolios
- Want income return that can complement development pipeline (Liverpool St / Moorgate and Packaged Living)
- 10 year plus leases, not many lease events in next 3-4 years, happy to take on OMRRs
- Targeting +6-7% unlevered IRR
- Used to be 5 year holds, more flex in this now
- Sector agnostic, although looking to reduce exposure on offices
- Can do pre-let forward fundings, but no spec dev
- Assess location on deal by deal basis, not as constrained as they once were
- Long income
  - Focus is income strips and ground rents 
  - Annuity book is in buy mode at the moment
  - Ground rents can do 15% gearing
- Overweight in cash so unlikely to be sellers

name: Boltbee Brooks
comments:
•	Largely in sales mode at the moment, once done Boultbee will go again on acquisitions
•	May have some money for assets in distress
•	Also looking at stock where they can be DM – deck attached
o	Strong ESG focus - invested in solar farm and energy storage facilities sites across the UK with a pipeline in excess of £1.5bn.
•	They will pay intro fees on DM kit
•	Could tie up with Pave if there is a DM angle
•	Interested in seeing Biggin Hill (sent)

name: Bravo Investments
comments: Commercial, hotel. £5-£25mn. M25 or crossrail

name: Brookfield Asset Mgmt
comments:
Met George just now with RBS, he controls London and is the one to speak to as opposed to Dominic which is probs why we aren’t getting a response….Dominic runs pan European and retail parks.

Anyhow, as we knew, they want opportunistic stuff in the Wet End, any sector, minimum lot size £50m. They bid on Langham yesterday…. @Woody Bruce Gibbo was acting for them on the offices did you do the retail for him in the end?? They were looking at a ground lease on Outernet interestingly?!?

They obviously love RBS and the leasing team so happy to be guided by us on the retail and where to target.

name: CBRE IM
comments:
16.11.2022 
Cap Markets WIP notes - John Quere (Director, Transactions)
•	Team structure: Tom James leads transaction function in UK, and Jon Quere (Director) supports him
•	Role of team is to support capital markets business to originate and execute transactions
o	UK Discretionary Capital - all equity
o	Inbound capital - pooled funds from across business, European / global separate accounts
•	Beds and sheds sector is primary focus - opportunistic requirements in other sectors
•	Global Alpha is CBRE's flagship global fund, GAV c. £10bn - have allocated 300m in equity to invest in UK (unlikely to take debt at present)
o	Residential and core plus shopping centre is key focus - can do direct but not afraid of minority or majority JVs
o	Targeting double digit returns
•	Pan-European Core Fund (PEC)
o	Core logistics, West End prime offices, residential (stabilised and forward commit - not fundings at present)
o	£300m equity for UK, plus extra from debt
o	Revolving green debt facility with fixed low interest rate
o	Targeting core returns between 6 and 7% unlevered returns
•	ELP (European Logistics Properties) - formed by merger of 2 existing CBRE IM funds
o	Raised 6-700m of equity to invest in Europe, 200m for UK
o	Targeting spec logistics development, return requirements value add, late single digits over ten year period
o	Quite active now that logistics land pricing has come off
o	GDV >50m, build-to-core strategy in top-tier locations. Will form JVs with best in class operators.
•	All 3 funds above are ready to invest today
•	European Residential Impact Fund 
o	Newly formed
o	£700m of equity to invest across Europe
o	£50m per year next 5 years, London and key cities
o	As with PEC, fundings difficult at present - stabilised and forward commits favourable
o	£30-100m lot size, core/core+
•	EPF (Malaysia)
o	Malaysian restrictions mean cannot convert MY currency into GBP so not able to invest at present
o	However, expected to lift this restriction in next few months - keen to invest
o	Return requirements have come down, so may be competitive
o	Monitoring offices (Central London) and logistics
•	Within UK, split into two buckets:
o	Conventional - £700m of requirements in UK made up of 3 or 4 key clients
•	Hampshire - opportunity-led, £200m to invest as at today, stabilised resi, PBSA, core logistics
•	West Midlands - opportunity-led, full on offices, easier return requirement than Hampshire so less risk needed
•	Unilever PF - 100% best-in-class ESG assets (or opportunities to create these assets), offices and keen to see stock in beds sector
•	UK Affordable Housing Fund - £200m equity to invest today, plan is to continue to grow the fund as quickly as possible
o	Long Income - £200m to invest across 2 or 3 key clients
•	Supermarkets, logistics, and possibly leased hotels
•	To summarise, significant amount of capital to invest across business - opportunity-led but patient.

name: Cerberus
comments:
Steven Clegg and Lorenzo Martinengo, both MDs in the European Real Estate Team.

•	Cerberus operates across credit, private equity and real estate platforms
•	Their real estate invests in debt and equity and has invested a total of $29bn to date
•	Cerberus’ residential focus covers Europe and the UK
•	Specifically interested in UK PBSA and UK Senior Living
•	They have recently considered backing a UK PRS platform
•	They have also looked at shared ownership but nothing has materialised yet
•	Key questions from our initial call focussed around:
o	Existing debt on the portfolio
o	How the seed portfolio has been funded
o	How AHH is taking cash out of the platform
o	The level of amenities provided at the schemes

name: Chu (HK)
comments:
1. Preferably Freehold 
2. ⁠Single tenant 
3. ⁠Preferably located in a major tourist attraction in the west end 
3. ⁠with over 5 years to expiry and tenant has no break option
4. ⁠zone 1
5. ⁠NiY over 6% with upside 
6. ⁠upward rent review only 
7. ⁠close to main underground stations

name: Clearbell
comments: We can go up to £50m on London offices however would prefer if the property was income producing and not VP.

name: Cohabs
comments:
July 2023 (React News): Co-Living platform funded by Ivanhoe Cambridge. 2,000 units in NY, Paris, Brussels. Now looking at London. Focusing largely across zones one and two in London, with an emphasis on schemes of below 100 bedrooms, and a sole focus on refurbishment of existing buildings rather than development projects. 

•     Existing buildings of 3,000 - 10,000 sqft 
•     Zones 1 - 2 London.  Preferred boroughs of Southwark, Lambeth, Wandsworth, Hammersmith & Fulham, Camden, Islington, Hackney and Tower Hamlets 
•     Ability to undertake extensive renovations: maximise unit layout on floor-plan, add amenity spaces, rear and loft extensions. 
•     Freehold 
•     Fast approval process with 100% cash purchase 
•     The opportunity must have planning for either:   
o     C4 (large HMO) or Sui Generis HMO buildings of 15+ existing bedrooms (can be shared bathrooms or self-contained) 
o     C3 residential blocks of 4+ flats, not located in an Article 4 area.

name: Crosstree
comments:
Crosstree is a private real estate investment firm, with a focus on the UK
•	Historically, their capital was exclusively from private wealth (Ernesto Bertarelli’s family office)
•	Their investor pool has since expanded to include institutional investors and other family offices
•	Have looked at both retirement and affordable. Yet to invest into the space but definitely of interest
•	Have looked at the higher end of seniors, Pegasus in particular
•	Have looked at buying an affordable developer/builder in the past
•	Historic London focus but looking at wider national geographies for the living sectors

name: Darlington Partners
comments: Single shop 8%+

name: Dorrington
comments:
•	Buildings with VP or short term income 
•	Suitable for serviced office implementation (we look at the floor plate, where the core is situated and window line) 
•	30,000 sq ft plus. 
•	Core Zone 1 locations 
•	Would look at West End but can’t be too expensive
•	Capital value has to be low enough to warrant a £500 psf plus spend on fit out. 
•	Good ‘bones’ of building

name: Estama
comments:
Nov 24:
Saw Ross earlier:

•	Main mandates continue to be with Signal Capital and MDSR (Israeli money).
o	Looking to bid on Festival Place, Basingstoke with MDSR. There appears to be good interest in this.
o	MDSR want big >£75-100m stock and are mandated by CBRE, so will be hard to get into them.
•	David Roberts (old Edinburgh House) mandate continuing to look at high street and shopping centres (£4-£12m / double digit yields).
o	Recently bid on something in Southsea which they liked.
o	Also bid on something in Taverstock.
•	Focus fund (owns Glenrothes and Wrexham) – considering exit strategy but C&W aware.
•	Other stock:
o	Beaumont Shopping Centre, Leicester – seem to think this won’t sell.
o	Workington – Evolve buying / have bought.
•	Generally struggling to compete with the likes of Evolve on pricing.

July 23
 •	Estama have around 7-10 staff – have a sister company that operates in Germany (property brokerage).
•	Have a JV with Signal Capital, as well as a couple of other JV partners. 
•	Will look at high street and SCs in good, supply constrained towns. Likely to be more secondary. Own stuff in Taunton, Caerphilly. Not anything in London. 
•	Won’t buy retail warehousing, or in towns where there is a high provision of retail (in-town or out of town).
•	For their own money, target lot sizes are around £2-5m. Anything over will need a JV partner. 
•	Recently bid on something up to £80m with JV partner. Underbidder on Livingstone SC (£55m). 
•	Bought Thamesgate in Gravesend last year: https://reactnews.com/article/signal-completes-thamesgate-shopping-centre-deal/
•	Sold out of most their old Edinburgh House stock.

name: Euro Car Parks
comments:
17/07/2023: Jason Tucker - Euro Car Parks
In growth mode. Have team across the country that can look at sites speculatively. Have track record in boosting income in car park. Also put in cameras etc. Shoreditch (for example) put on activation events to boost car park, food events etc. 
Will look at commercial (out of town offices with short income) if there is a parking angle (c. £5m). Will take a view on leasing / capex risk. Eg bought derelict building in Dunfermline to get parking. 
Prefer mngt agreements rather than lease. 
Can be brought in on a short term income to operate parking. Eg on development site awaiting planning.

name: FM Investment
comments:
I now have an established client A who is prepared to pay 2% buyers commissions. He would like to acquire a London zone 1 retail shop with heavy footfall and with NIY north of 5%. He will consider buying either freehold or a virtual freehold property but not something with less than 500 years remaining unexpired. His budget is between GBP 3.0 to 5.0 Million.
 
I have another established client B who is shopping for a retail shop ideally within London zone 1 but preferably still within zone 3 (with NIY north of 5.0%) but will also consider principal county towns in southern England (provided the NIY is in excess of 6.0%). His ideal budget is between GBP 1.0 to 2.0 million.
 
I have a third established client C who is after either a boutique hotel or a luxury blocks of flats in a prime area in the West End of London. Their budget is up to GBP 50 Million. They will consider buying the hotel with hotel management in place (but with break options that would be within the next 5 -10 years). NIY should be respectable and preferably north of 4%. Ideally , if you come across a distressed sale, that would be perfect since they are prepared to pay 100% in cash with short completion time. 
 
I also have a fourth established client D who is only interested in international Grade A office bldgs. in the West End or in the City. FYI, they currently own one of the following three trophy bldgs. in the City namely the Walkie Talkie, the cheesegrater or the Gerkin. They would like to buy a fire sale office bldg. to balance their current portfolio since their current portfolio comprised some Grade A office bldgs. acquired at the height of the market back in the 2010s. They have no stated budget but anything smaller than GBP 100 Million would be too small to wet their appetite.

name: Fabrix Capital
comments:
Fabrix Sustaainable Communities -
•	PGP/Patrizia Global Partners (formerly Sparinvest) is a funds business acquired by Patrizia in 2018 and based in Copenhagen. They manage five global real estate funds with capital commitments of approx. €2 billion, all invested with/via local operators. PGP has a separate investment team and 100% investment autonomy from the main Patrizia Immobilien business.
•	PGP completed their final close on a new European Sustainable Communities fund totalling €500m commitments, mainly from European pension funds. The strategy is led by Marleen Bikker-Bekkers out of the Amsterdam office. https://reactnews.com/article/patrizia-closes-e500m-european-social-housing-fund/
•	Fabrix and PGP have formed a JV to invest in real estate in London, with the initial commitments for this vehicle to be funded from PGP’s latest fundraise, with an initial €125m earmarked for the JV. 
•	The JV is an impact vehicle and the target returns of the strategy are ESG-linked, with the headline goal to increase the supply of sustainable community infrastructure and affordable housing in London. Fabrix’s track record of community engagement, success at planning and expertise in both sustainable development and on-the-ground social impact meant we were natural partners for this strategy.
•	Our target purchases are where we can provide long-term investment into existing real-estate to improve and develop new assets of community value and residential space and improve the environmental performance.

name: Fiera
comments:
His main mandate at the moment is on behalf of an Israeli source. 
•	Seeking double digit IRRs across resi, industrial and offices.
•	Interested in soon to be or recently PC’d industrial and taking on leasing risk – I have contacted an old developer client from KF to see if there is any angle here on their scheme in Braintree:
o	https://www.horizon120.com/
•	Have looked at vacant Central London stock (although as we know their previous attempt failed IC).
•	More likely to do gritty standing stock where there might be an opportunity to unlock development / refurb.

Nick also looks for deals on behalf of Fiera’s long income funds, although they aren’t up to much at present.

Separately, Fiera also have their value add fund and logistics development fund. This is primarily in place to support their prop co’s like Package Living, Wrenbridge, Angle Property etc.

Fiera’s debt team have x2 pools of capital – Middle Eastern and Fiera balance sheet. They are busy but the lending market is very competitive. They are hopefully going to do their first deal from Fiera’s balance sheet soon.

name: Foundation Capital
comments:
Joint venture with Bright Bay Real Estate, backed by Starwood
- They are actively seeking industrial, logistics, or storage sites. In Park Royal and West London, they are open to sites as small as 2 acres, while for the rest of the M25 area, they prefer sites of 5 acres or more, and 7 acres or more elsewhere in the UK.
- Their strategy involves underwriting the current use of the site and then working to secure power and planning for a data centre as the value-add opportunity.

name: Glenstone
comments:
Nick and I saw Sam Russell from Glenstone this morning.

•	Glenstone recently merged with a prop co as part of capital gains tax strategy – most of the stock likely to be sold via auction (if sold).
•	Also own Alternative Income REIT - buying the Virgin Active, Streatham.
o	Owns a lot of Travelodges.
•	Generally, sounds like they are willing to buy anything that is a good deal.
•	Their portfolio has a low leverage around 20% gearing. Have a facility with Handelsbanken and Lloyds and can draw down straight away.
•	Sweet spot is £2-3m, but can go up to £5m for the right thing.
o	Don't need IC sub £3m.
•	Needs to be quite high-yielding.
•	They don't like to get their hands too dirty on AM / value-add.
•	Selling a shed in Leeds at a keen yield as it has a high reversion. Incoming purchaser to take on rent review ageing building risk.
•	Interested in buying kit in Central London at low cap vals.
•	Like retail parks but becoming expensive.
•	Track record in buying pubs – bid on the Franco Manca block in the City (bid end of last year).
o	Keen to see more.
o	Own some Stonegate kit.
•	@Nick Halley to send Pizza Express, Lavender Hill and Plymleigh House, Plymouth.
•	@Jack Barratt – do you know what happened to the Revs in Cardiff? Might be worth an intro if still available.

name: Global Mutual
comments:
09/01/2025
Car Park Portfolio
•	Historically, the portfolio consisted only of NCP assets and was owned by Davidson Kempner. Greenpoint bought this portfolio from DK (and now realise they overpaid).
•	Global Mutual manages the portfolio on behalf of Greenpoint. The portfolio mostly comprises NCP assets but now includes other operators.
•	The portfolio is split into ‘core’ and ‘non-core’ assets. 
o	Core Assets: Focused on Greenpoint’s strategy of expansion, implementing EV chargers and/or potentially last-mile logistics.
o	Non-Core Assets: May be sold to fund capex on the core portfolio. These assets include NCP-let car parks in secondary locations such as Colchester and Ipswich, with 16 years unexpired leases. As part of a Covid-era regear with NCP, each lease includes a landlord-only break on three months' notice, opening the possibility of conversion to alternative uses. 
•	Global Mutual / Greenpoint is keen to keep acquiring ‘core’ car parks.
•	If anyone has ideas for buyers who could acquire the non-core portfolio in bulk, let me know. I did say the market would be thin for this...

Urban Pubs & Bars Portfolio
•	Davidson Kempner and Global Mutual acquired Urban Pubs and Bars (UP&B) in 2021. This strategy focuses on investing in high-quality UK hospitality assets, concentrated in London and the South East. It is predominantly a leasehold operating business.
•	Brands: Includes the Salt Yard Group (Spanish and Italian tapas), Bat & Ball (sports bar and games hall), and pubs like The Wheatsheaf in Tooting and The Cyclist in Balham. It also operates the "Urban Locals" format in urban villages such as Highgate and three City bars.
•	Expansion: In August last year, UP&B acquired 11 new sites, including eight trading leasehold businesses and three vacant freehold sites from the administrators of Elflock Limited, Babel Silk Limited, Brocade Limited, and De Gremio Limited. 
o	The Bat & Ball site in Stratford serves as a blueprint for further national expansion.
•	Other Key Sites: The East Dulwich Tavern (East Dulwich), The Antelope (Tooting), and The Sun (Camberwell).
•	They remain acquisitive for this strategy and plan to grow the concept.

Tavern Propco Pub Portfolio
•	Davidson Kempner bought 370 pubs previously owned by Ei Group (formerly Enterprise Inns). The majority of these are owned freehold with ‘free of tie’ (FOT) leases.
•	Most sites are independent ‘watering holes’.
•	They remain acquisitive for this strategy.

Overarching Goal
The main objective for these portfolios is to build platforms and then exit. For instance, Global Mutual is considering exiting the core car park portfolio by listing it as a REIT.

Additional Opportunities
•	Global Mutual is also able to provide debt, although they acknowledge the market is quite crowded.
12/07/2023: Met with Christian Gomez from Global Mutual.  Interested in Biggin Hill, although will want to flip on PC rather than hold so unlikely to work. I will send when back at my desk. 
Buying Livingstone RP with Patron, not yet exchanged / completed. 
Looking at LDO, did not say who with but presumably Patron (Daniel Weisz).
Carling (one of their capital sources) looking to exit UK. GM trying to line up investors for their assets (Patron likely) and recapitalise, rather than sell. These include 4 retail outlets. From memory they also own an office scheme in Cardiff. 
Greenpoint acquired their NCP car park portfolio, which they were AM on on behalf of Davidson Kempner. GM retained as AM. Greenpoint looking to acquire more car parks. Any ideas here?
Have 5 PBSA assets, 2 of which failed to sell, where they were brought in by the lender (Wells Fargo? TBD) as AM. CBRE and KF failed the sale. 
Retained as AM on ex-Intu schemes. Unlikely to sell these. Refi next year and carry out 3 year business plan.

name: Grosvenor
comments: London, Manchester, Birmingham and Edinburgh

name: HOOPP
comments:
28/03/2023:  
Eric has been at HOOPP 2 years
Focuses on industrial, offices, retail, resi (BTR and PBSA)
Half of their book is in Canada, 12% is in Europe, target for Europe will be no more than 10-15% of their book approximately 85% direct holdings, 15% indirect (funds)
Will take development risk – have a $4bn development pipeline
artners include Tishman Speyer, Invesco, Greystar and The Crown Estate one of their projects was 1 Eagle Place, so they will likely want to open an office here. They have a team of 18 investment professionals
and want to open a London office next year

Dislikes:
Uninterested in seniors due to potential reputational risk and havent spent a lot of time looking at co-living. Won’t take planning risk as they ‘can’t speculate’ won’t get involved in things like proptech.

31/05/2022: 15% of portfolio in Europe of which 85% direct (JVs mostly), 15% funds. 
Target 10 to 12% returns but can do deals either side. Overall 30% leverage across books, typically 50% on a deal. Min 5% equity, ideally more up to 50:50. 
Focus on growing industrial into strategies not covered by Veridion (Big box, UK, Sweden, Denmark, Germany). 80% of european book is logistics. 
Back Greystar and Invesco for resi, likely more Greystar going forward. Including UK, Spain, Ireland, Netherlands. Prefer to back operators/developers. 
Open to London offices but no primary focus. Prefers programmatic, tied to existing partners unless opportunity is compelling. Looking to open London office 2023. Current office partners are Crown Estate and Helical. Mentioned global relationship with Tristan (funds/coinvest), and with AXA for developments.

name: ICG
comments:
09/01/2023: •	ICG Longbow - historically UK debt
•	Launched sale and lease back fund and now raising value add/opportunistic fund
•	c.$7bn AUM on the real estate side

ICG formulated an offer for Edmonton in 2018. 
21/09/22 - Historically core plus investors in their first fund. Their first fund invested in S&L assets - mission critical assets. From residential in the Baltics to logistics in the UK, and supermarkets in Europe. Done well historically through their credit background – can get comfortable on tenants where others may not. Deals include Jaguar Land Rover campus and Royal Mail, Edinburgh. Struggle with offices and high street retail. 
New opportunistic capital with new fund raise. Being more thematic (logistics and light industrial). Looking at food retail vehicle and niche mission critical assets (eg Wembley Arena). Target returns of double digit IRRS / stabilised cash on cash of 5-7%

name: KF-IM
comments:
•	Harry works across transactions and fund / asset mngt
•	Works on the Lancashire PF. Target c.£10m lot sizes. Product needs to be located in Lancashire. Not much money to spend atm. 13 assets in the portfolio. 
•	Also works on one of KFIM’s pooled PF mandates (capital from Lancashire CC PF, Berkshire CC PF and a London LA PF). UK-wide mandate. £20-30m target lot size. Will do forward fundings. Buying Lidl and McDonalds atm [TBD if it was for this mandate], and another high street block. Money to spend but unlikely to be retail, perhaps sheds / alternatives. Will be buying in Q4. 
•	Middle Eastern mandate - opportunistic buyer. Harry referenced this mandate as ‘bottom feeders’. Wants value-add offices. Recently bought an office in the West End. Also bought in St Albans and Edinburgh. Looking for distress.
•	New, separate mandate with Berkshire CC PF following the success of Lancs CC PF. Product has to be in Berkshire. Have money to spend. Circa £2-10m lots. 
•	Long income fund has some sales coming. 
•	Discussed AM fees - their mandates work off income and asset value. Depends on how the fund is set up. Thinks charging AM fees off income better, although referenced this was not the case during Covid as no rent was collected. 
•	Discussed Biggin Hill – Bombardier credit won’t be good enough for their long-income fund, but asked to have a look to see if they can find another home for it.

name: KZN Real Estate
comments:
Aug 23 - The KZN Real Estate team believe every day presents the opportunity to improve. We apply this belief to every project and investment opportunity, in a constant pursuit of driving superior returns for partners. KZN Real Estate was established to create & deliver real estate investment value and performance, by capitalising on the economic opportunity offered by ESG. By taking assets through the 'Brown to Green Journey', we believe superior returns will be consistently delivered, and most critically, delivered in the 'right way'. We invest our own funds alongside joint venture partners to acquire real estate across all asset classes, adding value through strong stock selection, rigorous due diligence and a continuous search for improvement. We seek to work with new and established partners who share our ambitions to deliver best-in-class assets and align with our focus on sustainability. 
Sep 6th - 
•	Given his background from NFUM, he’s looking to build a balanced portfolio across the main property sectors from spec industrial to high street.
•	Target lot size £10-£20m.
•	IRR, value-add driven and will utilise debt.
•	Needs to have ESG-improvement angle, and needs to have an institutional exit in mind.
•	Happy to do heavy lifting on refurbs.
•	Sits in Colmore Capital’s office in Birmingham – I think he has some capital from them and is able to use their asset management / development capability as a resource.
•	Seeking further sources of capital commitments to help grow the portfolio, and is speaking to privates and family offices to help with this.
•	He would be able to do Sainsbury’s, Reading with existing capital, but anything larger he would need to seek further capital.

name: LaSalle Investment Management
comments:
Aug 23 - They are selling their Premier Inn in Richmond from their Total Pension Fund. Under offer to CBREIM. Lot size around £11m. Has been under offer for 18 months due to cladding issues. Costs to rectify cladding issues factored into CBREIM offer and novated to them so that LaSalle have a clean exit. 

Selling Tortilla and one other shop in Reading to KFIM (for their Berkshire PF). WAULT approx. 10 years. Sam Waterworth acting for KFIM. 

Buying a PBSA scheme in Lincoln (CBRE acting for them). Long income play to the uni. 

Generally focussed on long income (>15 year income with RPI uplifts). 

Looked at Biggin but put off by the metrics. 

Have value add money looking at living sectors, life sciences and sheds. 

Total and Santander PF (that Chris works on) unlikely to be doing much. Generally pension funds are struggling to meet redemptions and the lack of liquidity in property is causing them issues. Therefore they are more likely to look at bonds, equities etc. The same will be true across all other UK PFs, who will continue to be net sellers.

They have seen Debenhams in Oxford. They aren’t looking at it as they are developing the old Boswell Book shop with Reef (opposite the Debs).

name: Longmead
comments:
Longmead Capital: Overview and Investment Profile
Who They Are: Longmead Capital is an independent investment manager specializing in UK real estate and infrastructure investments. Founded in 2014, the firm operates with a proactive and research-driven approach, managing funds on behalf of family offices, high-net-worth individuals, and institutional investors. The team has extensive experience in direct real estate and sale-and-leaseback agreements, with a notable deal history including transactions in sectors such as car parks and logistics.

Active Requirements and Interests:
1.	Car Park Portfolios:
o	Longmead owns a large Q-Park portfolio and is familiar with market dynamics, including portfolios previously brought to market.
o	Interested in single-let car parks, particularly those with alternative use potential.
2.	Office Properties:
o	Interest in offices with narrow floor plates, as these tend to provide flexibility for single-let use or specific tenants.
o	They find multi-let office buildings with larger floor plates more challenging.
3.	Open Storage:
o	Tend to lease open storage to tenants.
o	Pension fund money backs this strategy so needs income return.
4.	Corporate M&A:
o	Actively seeking M&A opportunities in the logistics and haulage sectors, including businesses like Eddie Stobart or port-related companies.
5.	General Approach:
o	May engage agents for acquisitions but also comfortable pursuing direct deals.

Sources of Capital: Longmead’s capital comes from a diversified pool, including:
•	Family offices
•	High-net-worth individuals
•	Institutional investors

Their investment capacity and strategy align with conservative underwriting and risk-adjusted returns.

name: McTaggart Family & Partners
comments:
03/02/2023
MF&P is the co-investment management platform of Western Heritable.
A long-established Family Office backed real estate private equity firm, Western Heritable focuses on adding value to real estate and creating core assets to be held for the long term.
Western Heritable will always be a significant equity investor in every transaction the platform undertakes and stand alongside co-investors.
Principal commercial investment portfolios are in London and New York.
Whole portfolio geared towards income generation but slightly further up the risk curve.
Use debt / third party capital on deals – confirmed they use debt advisors on deals
Historically interested in resi:
Where they started (affordable development in Scotland)
No current strategy in resi, prefer operational real estate
Co-Living – nearly invested in Mason & Fifth but decided against it (and are glad they didn’t)
Current interests / requirements:
Key strategy – hotels
Not yet seen distress in the hotel market
Fully owner operated / owned portfolio – x4 in London (Victoria, Kensington, Covent Garden, Soho), x1 in Liverpool, x1 (under construction in Edinburgh), x1 in planning in Farringdon
Total portfolio = 600 keys
All assets were office buildings where they took planning risk as a way of boosting equity multiple
Last year acquired a majority stake and operational responsibility for another hotel group = 400 keys
Total portfolio = 1,000 keys
Target is 2,500 keys in 5-10 years
Hotel in Covent Garden is #1 in the UK (EBITDA conversions of around 40%)
Have a solid brand and needs scale (up to a point) – max 150 keys per asset as otherwise it begins to lose boutique / city centre feel
In conversations with another hotel group that they are looking to acquire
Equity cheques can get big and don’t have the equity to do all of it
Therefore potential to raise capital for large share transaction
Would need the right term / deal etc
Key strategy – self-storage
Core strategy – v-a office buildings in London (Farringdon, St James’s) and spreading into Thames Valley (Basingstoke)
Not currently looking to add to London office portfolio
New strategy - tired offices on business parks in reasonably affluent locations (currently have a holding in Fleet, looked at Leatherhead), with a view to demolishing a building sheds
Like JVs and have done a lot of them - would often be the GP as they like control.

23/11/23
Update below from the McTaggarts:

Hotels
o	Farringdon (incl. Long Lane) – originally they JV’d with the adjacent owner (GMS Estates?) for a 3-building refurb / redev. McTaggart have since changed their mind and are now going in for planning to a hotel conversion. Around 140 keys and McTaggart will operate under The Resident brand.
o	They have got their hands full on their hotel schemes, with a new opening in Edinburgh in the not too distant future. 
o	Strategy on pause for the next 6 months.
•	Value Add offices – they have teamed up with Hugo Denee (formerly of Stepstone, now at ‘Longstock Capital’) to carry out AM / DM work on offices in Basingstoke and Bristol.
o	Canons House, Bristol - https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hugo-den%C3%A9e-b97a6825_bristol-bristolproperty-greenoffice-activity-7092130566082310146-d3fT/
o	Plant, Basingstoke (formerly Mountbatten House) - https://www.plantinbasingstoke.com/
•	Self storage
o	Looking to grow out this strategy – they are after VP / short income offices in urban locations. The game plan is to grow a portfolio.
o	They like the idea of having co-work space within the self storage facility.
o	Some cross-over with their offices strategy – they like markets like Guildford, Brighton, Leatherhead etc where the office market is faltering and resi conversion is unappealing (i.e. the office building may be on or near a business park / industrial estate).

name: Newcore
comments:
July 2023 (React News): Newcore Capital has acquired seven assets for its £190m Newcore Special Situations V (NSS V) fund, including properties in several new subsectors. The acquisitions total £25m and are spread across Greater London and neighbouring commuter towns. The purchases include 2 petrol stations with EV charging in Crouch End and Streatham, and a mortuary in Surrey let to Dignity Funerals. This is the first time Newcore has invested in the roadside and funeral care sectors, driven by increasing demand for EV charging and the UK’s ageing population. As part of its latest round of acquisitions, Newcore has also purchased nurseries in Whitton, Beaconsfield and Clapham, and a special needs school in Horsham.

NSS V reached its final close in May after securing £190m in equity commitments. Investors include Merseyside and Clwyd local government pension schemes, two European fund-of-funds managers, insurance and corporate pension schemes, family offices and high-net-worth individuals. Newcore is aiming to deploy £350m over the next two years for NSS V, with target asset classes including education, healthcare, storage, life sciences and transport. Lot sizes are targeted between £2m and £25m.

name: Orchard Street
comments:
06/07/2023

	This is our summary. Targeting 12%-15% IRR – we can leverage if it’s accretive. 
	Summary for main sectors but we’re looking at care home repositioning, some garden centres and others so we’ll cover anything interesting. 
	 
	High level summary of the new OSIM SEIP investment requirement:
		· Sector agnostic
		· UK-wide requirement (although likely to larger or well-established urban centres)
		· c. £5m - £20m lot sizes on entry with heavy capex requirement
		· Short income (< 3 years) or Vacant Possession opportunities to ensure repositioning business plan can be executed 
		· Preference to refurbish and add to the existing real estate rather than full demolition
	
	Industrial/Logistics
		- Location: Edge of strong urban centres
		- Age: Typical 1980s/1990s sheds in need of heavy refurbishment but fundamentally decent kit
		- Single let: 50,000-100,000 sq ft / Good eaves / Decent yards required
		- Multi-let: Requirement to refurbish half the space within 2-3 years, leases outside the 54 Act
	 
	Retail Warehouses
		- Location: In urban centres with limited competition 
		- Age: Up to early 2000’s which require meaningful capex 
		- Single let: VP or short income opps where the tenant is leaving or we can engage them to refurbish unit
		- Multi-let: Requirement to refurbish/’green up’ half the space within 3-4 yrs
		- VP: Preference for leases outside the 54 Act but can be inside where we can engage with tenants through existing relationships
	 
	Offices
		- Location: Big 6 / Growth locations e.g. Brighton
		- Quality: Broken buildings in need of full refurbishment with fundamentally good bones; floor to ceiling
		- Planning: Upside from adding massing and roof terraces.
Need for VP within 2-3 years

name: Orpen Kisacikoglu
comments: The UBO is called Orpen Kisacikoglu, of Turkish origin but resident in the UK. Main group is set up in Malta. Specifically asked to look at indirect opportunities. Lot size would be small, $5m upwards, maybe $10m plus.

name: Oryx
comments:
Logistics development:

Strategy: acquisition of land to speculatively develop small / mid box logistics units in urban locations.
Location: Golden Triangle, the Big 6 and the Southeast / London.
Land size: up to 15 acres, depending on location e.g. smaller land size for Greater London and larger size for Golden Triangle.
All-in cost (acquisition plus capital expenditure): min – max of £16m - £35m.
Planning: consented or unconsented sites will be considered. Unconsented sites only considered if there is no material change of use.
Build: multi-unit or single unit to be considered.
 

Long income:

Strategy: acquisition of income generating assets, let to credit worthy tenants, on long leases.
Location: pan-UK with a preference for South East and Big 6.
Acquisition price: £12m - £45m.
Asset class: sector agnostic – offices remain challenging, but could work if the covenant, location, and tenant use are all satisfactory.
Net initial yield: min. 7.50% (this is subject to change given market conditions).
Lease length: minimum 12 years unexpired lease term.
Rent review: annual or five yearly inflation linked rent reviews. Open market rent review leases can be considered, but at a higher NIY.
 

Office repositioning:

Strategy: acquisition of office assets in strong occupational markets with the aim of adding value through capital expenditure and leasing.
Location: Central London, Greater London and South East.
Acquisition price: £10m - £20m.
All-in cost (acquisition plus capital expenditure): max. £30m.
Capital value: max. £750 psf. for Central London, max. £250 psf. for Greater London and South East.
Tenancy: multi-let or ability to be converted to multi-let property.
Lease length: max. unexpired average lease length of three years.

name: PIC
comments:
15/11/2024
PIC Overview and Investment Approach
•	PIC specialises in managing defined benefit pension liabilities.
•	Their preferred deal structure combines debt and equity. For example, in Build-to-Rent (BTR) schemes, they often allocate 50% as pension fund debt (e.g., from BP Pension Fund) and the remainder as IRR-driven shareholder equity, typically sourced from sovereign wealth capital from Kuwait. These investments target a 10–20 year horizon, with exits via recap or sale.

Investment Criteria
•	Sectors: Long income, living, and industrial.
•	Minimum lot size: £15m.
•	Long-income investments must be investment-grade and linked to CPI/RPI.
•	PIC values a long lead-in time for deal preparation and execution.

Potential Deal Opportunities
•	David Lloyd forward fundings
•	Q Park Mailbox
•	Brixton wrapper

PIC’s Key Projects
1 Ruskin Square, Croydon
•	£268m forward purchase of a Grade A office building leased to the Government Property Agency, targeting sustainability standards like BREEAM "Outstanding."
New Vic, Manchester
•	£130m BTR development near Manchester Victoria Station, featuring 520 apartments, commercial space, and resident amenities. 
Milton Keynes (Bowback House)
•	£80m BTR project with 306 apartments, including 43 affordable units. The development prioritises sustainability with features like solar panels and air-source heat pumps.
Octopus Real Estate
•	Key initiatives include the "Senior Living Investment Partners" JV, developing retirement living communities such as the St Albans scheme.

name: SevenCitiesLDN
comments:
SevenCitiesLdn is a newly formed property investment, development and asset management company, dedicated to acquiring and repositioning value-add real estate opportunities across London and top regional cities throughout the UK. Our focus is primarily on city
centres, with all uses considered - office, hotel, residential (BTR and private sale), student, leisure and retail.
SevenCitiesLdn is backed by SevenCapital Plc; a leading UK real estate group and specialist in urban regeneration, known for its strong track record of delivering high-quality residential and mixed-use developments across the UK. The business has a current portfolio in
excess of £2.1 billion, equating to over 7.8 million sq ft of floorspace.
Together we are committed to transforming brownfield land and undervalued property into thriving and sustainable communities.

name: Sovereign Centros
comments: Value Add shopping centres

name: Tellon Capital
comments:
Central London, Greater London & South East Offices
• Freehold & Long Leasehold (Max 7 0% rents receivable gearing and in excess of 7 05 years unexpired)
• Max 7 0 min walk to underground or mainline rail station
• £10m - £150m
• Short income to vacant possession or vacant possession
• Ability to refurbish or redevelop with or without planning

Industrial
• Freehold & Long Leasehold (Max 7 0% rents receivable gearing and in excess of 7 05 years unexpired)
• South of M6 and East of the M5
• £2m - £70m
• 30,000 sq ft - 100,000 sq ft
• Short income to vacant possession or vacant possession
• Ability to refurbish or redevelop with or without planning

Prime Retail
• Freehold & Long Leasehold (Max 7 0% rents receivable gearing and in excess of 105 years unexpired)
• Prime retail towns and cities with good quality connections
• South of Birmingham, as far west as Bristol and as far east as Chelmsford, Central London excluded
• £1 m - £70m
• Vacant possession, part vacant or leases with 7 8 months or less unexpired

•	They are continuing to look for value add offices in London. They recently looked at the Matalan on Oxford St and M&S [TBD Oxford St or Tottenham Court Rd]. 
•	Looking at Debenhams in Oxford, although require floor to ceiling height of 3 metres.
•	They have a resi scheme in Southampton (Bargate Qtr) that was being forward funded by L&G. Henry's were the contractor who have gone bust, so they are re-tendering for this. 
•	Bought and developed 16 Eastcheap with their Wealth Preservation fund. Unlikely to be acquisitive from this. Rupert and Lucy do the leasing on this. 
•	Most of their money comes from 12 or so family offices (assumed to be mostly from Israel). Main source is Ben Hamburger (Founding Partner), who is former CEO of Harel (Israel’s largest insurance group). 
•	Appetite to buy in London or cities with a good story. Need development / refurb angle and short hold to generate high IRRs to investors.  
Will also buy LH assets.

name: Threadneedle
comments:
July 2023 (Jenny): Aegon we intend to reposition the portfolio over the next couple of months disinvesting from the sectors which we feel won’t provide growth going forward, the majority of the high street retail is being dealt with via Graham Fawcett if you want to reach out to him if you have any ideas and the smaller secondary offices Max is reviewing at Kimmre. Backstop will be auction for most if not sold by YE. On the acq side we are on the hunt for industrial, retail warehousing and student accommodation. PBSA we don’t have are own operator but after direct let schemes. Forward fundings are tricky as we are income driven and don’t want our cash committed – up and built best for us on a focus of good russell group unis and good quality accommodation  

May 2023: Columbia Threadneedle Investments has formed a strategic real estate partnership with Aegon UK. The mandate, a commercial real estate portfolio valued in excess of €568.6m (£500m), will be managed by Columbia Threadneedle’s UK real estate team. The mandate will be led by Jenny Hewitt, Portfolio Manager, and James Coke, Co-Head Institutional Real Estate, supported by the 120 strong UK real estate team.

TREN AND TPUT main funds, pension funds only, £2.3bn last year and £1.7bn this year - driven by DB clients and valuation falls 
Working out how to plug gap that will be left by DB funds
TREN been selling significantly but no longer have redemptions 
Looking to increase cash in fund 
Investment managers going through seismic change on this front (end of DB schemes)

PE - 
Raising selected funds 1,2, 3 and select fund 4
"we raise funds like this when we think there will be dislocation / stress"
Preffered sectors are retail warehousing (specific underwriting model) multi-let industrial, strategic land 
Delivered mid teen net IRRs
Bearish on offices due to UK working patterns 
Money raised 1st asset u/o, 4 year period, 12-15% geared IRRs, 505% LTV, 5.5% all in debt cost

name: Tonsley Investments

name: Varde
comments:
2025/02/28

We’re not buying anything at the moment – focus is very much on credit (lending). Very happy to support any processes that you’re running or provide financing terms to support an acquisition (but deals need to be £100m+ ideally).

2023/05/26

Gary Roberts / Senior Advisor / groberts.advisor@varde.com/ +44 7787 550 847
	· 20 year career
	· Capital advisors at CBRE - 8/9 years 
	· Left in 2014 and 9 years COO of Praxis real estate focussing on opportunistic opportunities 
	· 2 weeks ago joined the Varde team - for UK and Ireland
 
Varde history
	· Historically PE and looking for distress 
	· Haven't done anything in UK/ Europe since 2018/2019
	· Inactive in the UK over the last few years have made a commercial lending book in the US
	· Bought Bizspace and bought with Crest Nicholson, which apparently did well
 
Varde now
	· Aim is to be a Capital solutions provider back sponsors and assets - not looking for distress 
	· Half a billion in the market in the next 12 months 
	· In the UK they think immediate opportunities will be lending  - gap funding / loan acquisitions 
	· Next phases is equity. They have the capital there if its right 
	· Returns need to be attractive 
	· Location agnostic
	· Sector agnostic - mentioned retail warehousing/ offices / self storage/ PBSA
	· Has doubts on shopping centres. Anything with department store vacancy is a challenge 
	· Nothing is an immediate no 
	· Mentioned the retail schemes in Yates and Luton being good deals
	· Varde have presence in Scandanavia / Southern Europe / UK and Ireland -  other locations also covered  within Varde  
	· Primary strategy without planning is a no 
	· Run two funds - very happy to go to 70% half paid half accrued 
	· Returns / Metrics 
			o Mid-teens IRRs
			o 70% debt loan / high single figure 8/9% deals
			o Preferred equity and structured equity can be done
			o Common equity needs to be 20+%
			o Ticket size north of £25m min - programmatic is challenging without sizeable deployment

name: Watkin Jones
comments:
Auug 23 -  
	· WJ are looking to increase cash on their balance sheet due to share price performance.
			o They are exiting sites that they have purchase options over (e.g. sites they have bought STP). 
			o They are also potentially looking to exit sites where they have demolished but not started construction. 
	· They are interested to hear from Nick Goodman re Asda Local (I have put Nick in touch). 
	· I suggested selling some of their GF commercial interests in order to raise cash - may be an angle here depending on location / asset quality. 
	· Looking to exit historic and poor performing PBSA kit (Sheffield and Droylesdon are a couple of the assets). Very poor assets with occupancy lower than 50-60%.
	· They have a vacant workspace in Stratford (opposite the station) – tenant / operator needs to be an affordable workspace to tie in with S106. 
 
Share Price and Recent Press
https://www.constructionnews.co.uk/sections/contractors/watkin-jones-ceo-departs-as-firm-flags-45m-exceptional-costs-19-07-2023/

name: Waypoint Asset Management
comments:
13/07/2023:  Money behind them is Willis Towers Watson and Schroders. Local retail fund - Schroders [TBD], Waypoint Essential Stores (retail Parks) - Willis Towers Watson [TBD]. May have more money for retail with convenience / food store in the future. Covered by Lee Randle unless off market. Buying retail parks - bought in Barrow and bought Bodmin off market from Orchard St. £5-15m lot sizee for retail parks. High street / convenience stuff more £5-10m. Unlikely to be acquisitive on Govt Income Fund due to gilts uncertainty at present. Kristyn going on maternity leave. On a few occasions on retail parks they have been under bidder to Realty Income. Realty haven’t performed so they have picked up. 

Value Add shopping centres

name: Weslyan
comments:
Freehold assets only 
- £5m to £20m lot size
- Throughout the UK
- Pre-let forward commitments 
- Target yield 5%+ 

Order of preference:
1. Industrial (single let, or several tenants, EPC B+, modern construction)
2. Alternatives (hotels, leisure, drive-through, education, healthcare exc. carehomes)
3. ‘ESG’ led assets – £2m+ (solar parks, EV charging hubs etc)
4. Retail Warehouse (solus or smaller parks)
5. London Offices

name: XLB
comments:
06/11/2024
Target Locations:
•	Key “cluster” cities with strong CBD/infrastructure, such as Oxford, Cambridge, Manchester, Bristol, Birmingham, Brighton, Southampton, and Milton Keynes.
•	Interested in London but may face pricing challenges.

Target Sectors:
•	Business Parks and City Centre Offices
•	Industrial, Self-Storage, Life Sciences

Asset Profile:
•	Focus on properties that are vacant or with short income, providing opportunities to add value in the short to medium term, as well as land development potential.

Project Specifications:
•	High-spec builds to attract skilled labour.
•	Preference for offices with green space to complement build quality.

Capabilities:
•	Asset and Development Management (new builds and refurbishments)
•	Master Planning (a growing area for them)
•	Capital Raising

Current Mandates:
•	Currently handling 8 mandates, backed by capital from AIMCO, Bridges, Pictet, Tristan, and KFIM, targeting IRRs of +15%.

Example Projects:
•	Sept 2024: Acquisition of Cody Technology Park, Farnborough (£112M) with Tristan Capital Partners in a sale-and-leaseback from QinetiQ.
•	Apr 2022: Surrey acquisition with Bridges Fund Management for a 90,000 sq ft logistics development.
•	Nov 2020: “The Powerhouse Portfolio” in Manchester with Pictet; refurbishment and ESG-focused management.
